title: Varanasi
summary: The oldest continuously inhabited city in the world and Hinduism's most sacred site — a place where every religion in India has sought presence and where death itself is understood as liberation, making it the city toward which Hindus across the subcontinent orient their lives and deaths.
hook: People come to Varanasi to die. Not because it is dangerous — because dying there is believed to release the soul from the cycle of rebirth. For three thousand years, a city has organized itself around this belief. It is still there.

beginner.thePlace: Varanasi — also called Kashi or Benares — sits on the western bank of the Ganges River in northern India, about halfway between Delhi and Kolkata. It is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world; people have lived there without interruption for at least three thousand years. The city is defined by its ghats — the long flights of stone steps that descend to the river — where people bathe, pray, wash clothes, and cremate their dead. Every morning, pilgrims come to the ghats before dawn to perform ritual bathing in the sacred river. Every night, priests perform the Ganga Aarti — an elaborate fire ceremony in honor of the river goddess — on the main ghats as crowds watch from boats on the river. The city smells of incense, flowers, and smoke.
beginner.physicalWorld: Varanasi's relationship to the Ganges is not simply geographic but cosmological. The river flows from the Himalayas southward and then turns north at Varanasi — running against the general direction of Indian rivers — before continuing east to the Bay of Bengal. This anomalous northward turn is understood in Hindu cosmology as the river rising toward the divine, and it is the physical basis for Varanasi's sacred geography. The city's eighty-four ghats line six kilometers of riverfront. The most sacred are Dashashwamedh Ghat (where Brahma is said to have performed a great sacrifice) and Manikarnika Ghat, the primary cremation ghat, where fires have burned continuously for millennia.
beginner.theLayers: Varanasi has accumulated layers of sacred presence like no other city in India. It is the most sacred city of Hinduism — where Shiva is said to reside permanently, making the city itself a form of divine presence. The Buddha gave his first teaching near Varanasi (at Sarnath, five kilometers away), making the area sacred to Buddhism as well. Jainism has important connections to the city. The Sufi tradition established a significant presence in the city under the Mughal empire. The weaving industry that Varanasi is also famous for — its silk brocades are the most prized in India — was built partly by Muslim craftsmen who settled in the city. The city is a palimpsest of sacred presences that have accumulated without fully displacing each other.
beginner.whatItBecame: Varanasi became the symbolic center of Hindu sacred geography — the tirtha (sacred ford) that connects the human world to the divine most directly. In Hindu cosmology, dying in Varanasi guarantees moksha — liberation from the cycle of rebirth — regardless of the moral quality of the person's life. This belief has drawn dying Hindus to the city for three thousand years, and it has organized the city's economy, its social structure, and its physical layout around the practice of death and cremation. The city has more than two thousand Hindu temples. The burning ghats operate around the clock. The priests who assist at cremations (the doms) belong to a specific caste that has managed the sacred fires for generations.
beginner.whoClaimsIt: Multiple religious traditions claim Varanasi, and their claims overlap rather than conflict. Hindus consider it Shiva's city — the place where the god resides permanently and where his presence makes death there liberating. Buddhists revere Sarnath, immediately outside the city, as the place of the first teaching. The Mughal emperors — particularly Aurangzeb — destroyed Hindu temples and built mosques on their sites, leaving a contested sacred geography that is still politically sensitive: the Gyanvapi mosque, built on the site of the original Kashi Vishwanath temple, remains a flashpoint of Hindu-Muslim tension in contemporary India.
beginner.theLongLife: Varanasi has survived invasions, religious conflicts, colonial rule, and modernization while maintaining its identity as a city organized around sacred presence and the practice of death. The physical fabric of the city — its narrow lanes, its ghats, its temples — has changed continuously while the basic function has remained stable. Contemporary Varanasi is also a significant university city (Banaras Hindu University, founded 1916, is one of India's largest), a silk weaving center, a center of classical music (the Banaras gharana is one of the major schools of Hindustani classical music), and an increasingly prominent tourist destination. The sacred city and the modern city coexist without resolving their tensions.

general.thePlace: Varanasi is the paradigm case of a sacred city — a place whose significance is entirely organized around the experience of the sacred rather than around commerce, administration, or military function. Its physical structure (the ghats, the temple network, the cremation grounds) is an architecture of religious practice rather than of secular function. Understanding Varanasi requires understanding how a city can be organized around a cosmological claim — that dying here liberates the soul — and how that organization has persisted for three thousand years across radical political changes, religious conflicts, and social transformations.
general.physicalWorld: The Ganges at Varanasi is not simply a river. It is a goddess (Ganga Ma) who descended from heaven through Shiva's hair, making the water simultaneously the river's physical substance and the divine presence itself. This dual nature is not metaphorical in the Hindu understanding; the water of the Ganges at Varanasi is materially the divine. Pilgrims immerse themselves in it to purify karmic accumulation; ashes of the dead are immersed in it to aid the transition between lives; the water is carried home in bottles to be given to the dying as they approach death. The physical purity of the water is simultaneously irrelevant (polluted water remains the divine substance) and deeply concerning — the Ganges at Varanasi is heavily polluted by industrial discharge, sewage, and partially cremated bodies, and the question of how to clean the river without desacralizing the practices that pollute it is one of the most intractable environmental and political problems in contemporary India.
general.theLayers: The layering of sacred traditions in Varanasi is architecturally visible. The Aurangzeb mosque (1669) sits adjacent to the current Kashi Vishwanath temple — the original was destroyed by Aurangzeb and rebuilt in the 18th century. The Buddhist site at Sarnath (five kilometers away) contains a remarkable archaeological site (the Dhamek Stupa, c. 500 CE), a museum with extraordinary Gupta-era sculpture, and a number of modern Buddhist temples built by different national Buddhist communities. The Sufi shrines (particularly the dargah of Kabir, the 15th-century poet-saint who synthesized Hindu and Islamic devotional traditions) occupy the same sacred geography as the Hindu temples. Each layer was added without fully erasing its predecessors, producing a city where multiple religious traditions are simultaneously present in the same physical space.
general.whatItBecame: Varanasi's identity as the city where death leads to liberation is not simply a religious belief; it is an organizing principle that has shaped the city's entire social structure. The doms — the caste responsible for managing the cremation fires — occupy a position of simultaneous pollution and power: their work is the most ritually impure in the Hindu system, yet without them the liberating fires cannot burn. The pandas — the priests who guide pilgrims through the ritual requirements of pilgrimage — have maintained genealogical records of pilgrims' families for centuries, allowing visitors to connect their current visit to ancestors who came to Varanasi generations earlier. The hospices (mukti bhavans) that receive dying Hindus who have come specifically to die in the city are organized around a specific theological understanding of what death here means. The entire city is a social institution organized around a cosmological claim.
general.whoClaimsIt: The contemporary politics of religious claims in Varanasi are among the most sensitive in India. The Gyanvapi mosque controversy — Hindu groups claim the mosque was built on the site of the original Kashi Vishwanath temple and demand its return — has been an ongoing legal and political battle since the 1990s and has intensified under the BJP government of Narendra Modi, who is a Member of Parliament from Varanasi. The construction of the new Kashi Vishwanath corridor (completed 2021), which opened the approach to the current temple, demolished several old structures and relocated residents, generating controversy about the relationship between religious heritage development and urban community. Varanasi is both the most sacred site in India and one of the most politically contested.
general.theLongLife: Varanasi's contemporary reality is the coexistence of the ancient and the modern in forms that neither fully accommodates the other. The sacred geography remains operative — pilgrims continue to come to die, the ghats continue to host cremations, the river continues to be bathed in. The city's silk weaving industry, centered in the Muslim-majority neighborhood of Madanpura, continues to produce the most prized textiles in India. The university brings students from across India. Tourism (including substantial international spiritual tourism) has added a layer of commercialization to the sacred landscape. The Ganges's pollution makes the ritual bathing that is central to the city's sacred function a genuine public health concern. None of these tensions has been resolved; they constitute the city's contemporary condition.

advanced.thePlace: Varanasi is the test case for Diana Eck's concept of the tirtha — the sacred ford — as the organizational principle of Hindu sacred geography. In Hindu cosmology, tirthas are places where the boundary between the human world and the divine is thin — where crossing is possible in both directions, where the divine can be approached most directly and where the human soul can most easily cross into liberation. Varanasi is not simply a sacred place; it is the sacred place — the tirtha par excellence, the point at which the entire sacred geography of Hinduism is most concentrated. Understanding this cosmological framework is prerequisite to understanding why the city has organized itself as it has and why the claim that dying here guarantees liberation has been taken seriously by tens of millions of people across three millennia.
advanced.physicalWorld: The paradox of the Ganges's pollution at Varanasi reveals a fundamental tension in the city's cosmological function. The river is simultaneously a material substance (water with measurable chemical properties, many of them dangerous) and a divine presence (the goddess Ganga, whose power transcends material properties). The ritual practices that make the city sacred — cremation in the river, bathing in the river, immersion of ashes — are also the primary sources of pollution that make the river dangerous. The Indian government's multiple failed attempts to clean the Ganges (the Ganga Action Plan, launched 1986; the Namami Gange program, launched 2014) have consistently underestimated the difficulty of separating the river's sacred function from the practices that pollute it. The pollution itself is the product of faith; the cleaning effort attempts to preserve the sacred experience while eliminating the material consequences of the practices that create it. The paradox does not have a technical solution.
advanced.theLayers: The layering of religious traditions in Varanasi has produced one of the most analytically interesting cases of sacred space competition in world history. The Gyanvapi mosque controversy involves not only competing religious claims but competing historical narratives (Hindu claims that the mosque was built on a destroyed temple; Muslim arguments about the legitimacy of existing structures; secular legal arguments about the status of religious sites under Indian law) and competing definitions of what historical justice requires. The controversy is not simply about one mosque and one temple; it is about the broader question of how contemporary India should relate to the religious conflicts of its Mughal past — a question that cuts to the heart of Indian national identity and the nature of Indian secularism. The physical space of Varanasi has become the terrain on which this constitutional question is being fought.
advanced.whatItBecame: Jonathan Parry's ethnographic work on death in Varanasi — his book Death in Banaras (1994) — is one of the most significant works of anthropology of religion produced in the late 20th century, and it significantly complicates the standard understanding of the city's sacred function. Parry found that the priests who manage the death rituals (the funeral priests, the doms) are themselves deeply ambivalent about the liberating power of death in Varanasi. The claim that dying in Varanasi guarantees liberation — regardless of moral quality — is understood by the priestly specialists as applying in full only under specific conditions that most dying visitors do not meet. The commercialization of the death rituals (the extensive fees extracted by funeral priests from dying visitors and their families) produces a cynicism that exists alongside genuine religious belief. The city that appears from the outside as a sacred space organized around cosmic liberation is, from the inside, a complex economy of sacred services whose practitioners have their own understandings of what the city's powers actually are.
advanced.whoClaimsIt: The Kashi Vishwanath corridor project (completed December 2021) represents the most significant physical transformation of Varanasi's sacred landscape in generations. Prime Minister Modi, who is the Member of Parliament from Varanasi, oversaw the project personally — its opening was a major political event, framed as the restoration of Hindu sacred space after Mughal desecration. The corridor demolished several old neighborhoods to create a processional approach to the temple, relocating thousands of residents. The project illustrates how sacred urban renewal serves simultaneously as religious restoration, political symbolism, and real estate development — how the three functions cannot be fully separated when the sacred space is also a political resource and an economic one. The transformation of Varanasi under the BJP government is one of the most consequential examples of contemporary Hindu nationalist sacred geography in practice.
advanced.theLongLife: The Banaras gharana — the school of Hindustani classical music associated with Varanasi — is a case study in how sacred cities produce secular cultural achievements through their concentration of traditional knowledge and patronage. The gharana's distinctive style (heavy use of thumri and dadra, close association between music and devotional practice, particular instrumental traditions) developed through centuries of court patronage and religious performance context. Ravi Shankar, Bismillah Khan (the shehnai master who played at every Indian Republic Day and who specifically requested that his ashes be scattered in the Ganges at Varanasi), and Girija Devi all came from or were deeply shaped by the Varanasi musical tradition. The city's sacred concentration produced, as a secondary effect, an extraordinary concentration of classical musical knowledge that has enriched Indian and world music far beyond the city's sacred function.

foundation.theStory: Varanasi is the clearest example in the world of a city whose entire organization — physical, social, economic, and political — has been shaped by a single cosmological claim: that dying there liberates the soul from the cycle of rebirth. Understanding Varanasi means understanding how a belief can organize a city across three thousand years of radical political change, how sacred geography works as a system, and how religious traditions compete for presence in shared sacred space.
foundation.theDebate: The central scholarly debate concerns how to understand the relationship between the city's cosmological claims and its social reality — whether the claim that dying in Varanasi guarantees liberation should be understood as a sincere religious belief, a social institution that serves other functions (economic, psychological, community-building), or a theological claim whose practitioners themselves understand differently from its official formulation. Parry's ethnographic complication of the standard picture is the central contribution to this debate.
foundation.whyItMatters: Varanasi matters because it is the paradigm case of a sacred city — a place whose significance is entirely organized around religious function rather than secular purpose. Understanding how such a city works illuminates the relationship between cosmological belief and social organization, shows how sacred space is contested and maintained across religious conflicts and political changes, and raises fundamental questions about how material reality and religious meaning relate to each other in the same physical space.
questions: Varanasi is organized around the belief that dying there guarantees liberation from the cycle of rebirth — regardless of the dying person's moral history. Jonathan Parry found that the priestly specialists who manage these deaths are themselves deeply ambivalent about this claim. What does this ambivalence suggest about how religious beliefs function in social institutions? Can a belief organize a social institution effectively even when the institution's practitioners are uncertain about its truth?

The Gyanvapi mosque in Varanasi was built on the site of a demolished Hindu temple in 1669. Hindu nationalists demand its return; the mosque has been in continuous use for over 350 years. What principles should govern how contemporary societies deal with sacred sites whose history includes religious destruction? Does the length of continuous use matter? Does the nature of the original destruction matter?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Varanasi scholarship spans religious studies, anthropology, urban history, and contemporary political analysis. Diana Eck's Banaras: City of Light (1982) is the foundational English-language scholarly treatment — rich in religious context and sacred geography but criticized for insufficient attention to social conflict. Jonathan Parry's Death in Banaras (1994) is the essential anthropological corrective — rigorously empirical, deeply engaged with the specialists who manage the sacred practices, and productively skeptical of the city's official self-representation. Christopher Bayly's Rulers, Townsmen and Bazaars (1983) covers the political economy of colonial Varanasi. Rana Dasgupta's Delhi: Adventures in a Megacity (2014) provides a comparative contemporary urban context.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The historical evidence for Varanasi's sacred status is deep but uneven. The city's antiquity is attested by archaeology (excavations at Rajghat have found continuous occupation from the 11th century BCE) and by textual sources (the Rigveda mentions Kashi). The layers of religious competition — the evidence for which temples were destroyed and when, the exact history of the Gyanvapi mosque — are documented but contested, with Hindu and Muslim sources providing incompatible accounts. The contemporary anthropological evidence (Parry, Eck, and others) is rich and rigorous but limited to specific dimensions of the city's life. The political history of the contemporary period — the BJP's transformation of the city's sacred landscape — is well-documented as current events but not yet fully integrated into scholarly analysis.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about Varanasi must hold the city's cosmological claims and its social reality in productive tension rather than reducing one to the other. The cosmological claim (dying here liberates) is not simply ideology masking social interests; it is a genuine theological position with a long philosophical history that organizes real behavior. The social reality (the economic interests of the funeral priests, the political uses of Hindu sacred geography) is not simply the "real" explanation underneath the religious surface; it coexists with the religious belief in the same individuals and institutions. The most productive analysis holds both simultaneously and examines how they interact.
questions: Diana Eck argues that the tirtha concept — sacred fords where the human and divine meet most directly — is the organizing principle of Hindu sacred geography, with Varanasi as the supreme tirtha. Compare this with how other religious traditions organize sacred geography (Jerusalem in Judaism, Christianity, and Islam; Mecca in Islam; Rome in Catholicism). What does the comparison reveal about the different ways religious traditions relate the divine to physical space?

Jonathan Parry found that the funeral priests of Varanasi are deeply ambivalent about the city's liberating power and have sophisticated theological understandings of the conditions under which liberation actually occurs — understandings that differ significantly from the popular belief that dying there guarantees liberation regardless. What methodological lessons does this finding suggest for the study of religious belief? How should scholars account for the gap between official doctrine and practitioner understanding?

research.topic: Varanasi as the oldest continuously inhabited city
research.content: Archaeological evidence from Rajghat confirms continuous occupation of the Varanasi site from at least the 11th century BCE, making it one of the oldest continuously inhabited urban settlements in the world. The city's prominence in the Rigveda and in Buddhist texts (which record the Buddha's first teaching at nearby Sarnath) confirms its significance from the earliest period of Indian literary history.

research.topic: Contemporary sacred landscape transformation under BJP governance
research.content: The Kashi Vishwanath corridor project (completed 2021) and the broader transformation of Varanasi under Prime Minister Modi's patronage represent a significant and ongoing transformation of the city's sacred landscape. Scholarly analysis of this transformation — its political dimensions, its effects on existing communities, its relationship to Hindu nationalist ideology — is emerging but not yet fully integrated into the longer scholarly literature on the city.

research.topic: The history of specific temple destructions and constructions
research.content: The contested history of sacred sites in Varanasi — specifically which temples were destroyed during the Mughal period, when, by whom, and on what sites mosques were subsequently built — cannot be definitively reconstructed from available evidence. Hindu and Muslim sources provide incompatible accounts motivated by contemporary political claims. What can be responsibly inferred: the Mughal emperor Aurangzeb ordered the destruction of Hindu temples in Varanasi during his reign (1658–1707 CE), including the original Kashi Vishwanath temple; mosques were subsequently built in the area. What must remain unknowable: the precise location of every destroyed temple, the exact sequence of construction and destruction events, and the degree to which the Gyanvapi mosque specifically was built on the site of the destroyed original temple versus in its vicinity.

comparativeNarrative.name: Kashi as Cosmic Center
comparativeNarrative.content: Within the Hindu pilgrimage tradition, Varanasi is not one sacred place among many but the sacred place — the point at which the entire sacred geography of Hinduism is most concentrated. The Sanskrit texts describe Kashi as the city that Shiva holds on his trident above the waters of cosmic dissolution — immune to destruction even when the universe is destroyed and recreated. Pilgrimage to Kashi is described in the texts as equivalent to visiting every other sacred site in India simultaneously. The city's eighty-four ghats are understood as corresponding to the eighty-four lakhs of life forms through which the soul passes in its journey toward liberation. The physical city is simultaneously itself and a map of the cosmos, in which every element corresponds to a cosmic reality. Dying here does not merely end one life; it dissolves the accumulated karma of all previous lives and ends the cycle of rebirth entirely.

comparativeNarrative.name: The City Near the First Teaching
comparativeNarrative.content: From the perspective of Buddhist historical memory, Varanasi is significant primarily as the city near which the Buddha gave his first teaching — the Deer Park at Sarnath, five kilometers away, where he taught the Four Noble Truths to five companions who became his first followers. This first teaching (the "First Turning of the Wheel of Dharma") is the founding moment of Buddhism as a tradition, and Sarnath is accordingly one of the four most sacred sites in Buddhism (alongside Bodh Gaya, Kushinagar, and Lumbini). The Buddhist relationship to Varanasi is thus historically prior — the Buddha's teaching at Sarnath predates the city's full development as a Hindu sacred center — but has been largely subordinated in the contemporary landscape, where Buddhist pilgrims visit Sarnath but the city itself is defined by Hindu practice. The relationship between Buddhist and Hindu sacred claims at this location is one of the more complex cases of religious co-presence in world history.

comparativeNarrative.name: Benares as Managed Sacred Site
comparativeNarrative.content: The British colonial experience of Varanasi — which they called Benares — was characterized by the tension between managing a sacred site that required administrative regulation and attempting to maintain the colonial pretense of religious neutrality. The British mapped the city, regulated the ghats, taxed the pilgrimage trade, and adjudicated disputes between Hindu and Muslim religious institutions — all while officially claiming not to interfere in religious matters. The colonial documentation of Varanasi produced extraordinary ethnographic records of religious practice (the surveys, the pilgrim records, the administrative correspondence) that are now among the most important sources for the city's late 18th and 19th century history. The British also funded and patronized the study of Sanskrit and Hindu religion at Banaras — Banaras Hindu University was founded with British support in 1916 — contributing to the very Hindu cultural revival that would eventually challenge colonial rule.

rabbitHole.label: Siddhartha Gautama
rabbitHole.reason: The Buddha gave his first teaching at Sarnath, immediately outside Varanasi — the city's sacred history is inseparable from the founding moment of Buddhism

rabbitHole.label: Jerusalem
rabbitHole.reason: The closest structural parallel — a city that is simultaneously the sacred center of multiple religious traditions, physically contested, and politically sensitive in contemporary geopolitics

rabbitHole.label: The Bhagavad Gita
rabbitHole.reason: The foundational text of the Hindu tradition that organizes Varanasi's sacred function — understanding the Gita's teaching about liberation (moksha) is prerequisite to understanding what dying in Varanasi is believed to accomplish

rabbitHole.label: The Gupta Empire
rabbitHole.reason: The Gupta period produced the classical Hindu and Buddhist art and architecture that defined Varanasi's sacred landscape — the connection between imperial patronage and sacred city development

rabbitHole.label: Law
rabbitHole.reason: The Gyanvapi mosque controversy pits religious claims against legal frameworks — the case is one of the most significant tests of Indian constitutional law's treatment of religious property rights

rabbitHole.label: Banaras: City of Light — Diana Eck (1982)
rabbitHole.reason: The foundational scholarly treatment of Varanasi in English — essential for understanding the sacred geography and cosmological framework that makes the city what it is

reference.author: Diana Eck
reference.title: Banaras: City of Light
reference.annotation: The foundational English-language scholarly treatment of Varanasi — rich in sacred geography, textual sources, and religious context. Eck's framework (the tirtha concept, the cosmological significance of the Ganges) remains the essential starting point. Criticized for insufficient attention to social conflict and political economy but irreplaceable as an introduction to the city's religious dimensions.

reference.author: Jonathan Parry
reference.title: Death in Banaras
reference.annotation: The essential ethnographic corrective to Eck's religious idealization — Parry's fieldwork with the funeral priests and doms of Varanasi reveals the complex social reality and theological ambivalence underneath the city's official sacred self-representation. Required reading alongside Eck for a complete picture.

reference.author: Christopher Bayly
reference.title: Rulers, Townsmen and Bazaars: North Indian Society in the Age of British Expansion
reference.annotation: Covers the political economy of colonial Varanasi — the relationships between sacred institutions, merchant communities, and colonial administration. Essential for understanding the economic dimensions of the sacred city.

reference.author: Rana Singh (ed.)
reference.title: Banaras: Making of India's Heritage City
reference.annotation: A collection covering multiple dimensions of Varanasi's history and contemporary condition — useful for specific topics (music, weaving, urban planning) but uneven in quality across chapters.

commerce.title: Banaras: City of Light
commerce.author: Diana Eck
commerce.note: Start here — comprehensive, beautifully written, and the essential framework for understanding why Varanasi is what it is

commerce.title: Death in Banaras
commerce.author: Jonathan Parry
commerce.note: Read alongside Eck — Parry's ethnographic realism provides the necessary corrective to the idealized picture

commerce.title: Varanasi (various travel and sacred geography documentaries)
commerce.note: The city's visual and sensory dimensions cannot be conveyed in text — any serious documentary filmed at the ghats, particularly during dawn bathing or the Ganga Aarti, provides irreplaceable sensory context for the written accounts

popularCulture.title: The Romantics — Pankaj Mishra (1999)
popularCulture.description: Mishra's novel is set in Varanasi and captures the city's contemporary condition — its coexistence of ancient sacred practice and modern intellectual life, its complex relationship to Indian nationalism, and the experience of it as both sacred site and living city for the people who actually inhabit it. One of the most nuanced literary treatments of contemporary India's relationship to its own sacred heritage.

popularCulture.title: Masaan — Neeraj Ghaywan (2015)
popularCulture.description: Ghaywan's film is set on and around the cremation ghats of Varanasi, using the city's sacred landscape as the backdrop for a story about caste, sexuality, grief, and the possibility of redemption. The film engages directly with the city's sacred economy (the dom caste that manages the cremation fires, the families of the dead, the ghat workers) and with the tension between the city's cosmological promises and the social realities of its inhabitants.

popularCulture.title: Bismillah Khan and the Varanasi musical tradition
popularCulture.description: The shehnai player Bismillah Khan (1916–2006) — who spent most of his life in Varanasi, performed at Indian independence in 1947, and specifically requested that his ashes be scattered in the Ganges at Varanasi — is the most famous representative of the city's classical music tradition. His recordings of morning ragas played at the ghats are among the most evocative documents of what it means to experience Varanasi as a sacred acoustic environment.
